translation: Sad. By the Qur'an containing reminder
commentary:
**صٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ذِى ٱلذِّكْرِ**

*Sad. By the Quran containing reminder.*

This surah marks the beginning of a profoundly important section of the Quran. Surah Sad contains eighty-eight ayahs and follows directly after Surah As-Saffat. The connection between these two surahs reveals something remarkable about the Quran's coherence. While scholars have long debated whether the Quran possesses structural coherence beyond its unified teachings, the continuations and subtle references from one surah to the next offer compelling evidence of deliberate arrangement.

We must understand that there are two distinct curricula in our relationship with revelation. The first is the chronological order of revelation, the sequence in which the Companions (RA) received the ayahs. They experienced the Quran passage by passage, sometimes just a single ayah, sometimes an entire surah, responding to specific situations as they arose. The second curriculum is the one Allah ﷻ arranged for all subsequent generations through the order we find in the Mushaf today. This arrangement, known as *tawqifi* (divinely instructed), was taught to the Companions (RA) by the Prophet ﷺ himself. This is the sequence in which they would recite the Quran, dividing it into seven sections to complete in a week.

The surah opens with the disconnected letter **صٓ** . Like other such letters in the Quran, this is not counted as a separate ayah but serves a distinct purpose. When we hear "Sad," our minds immediately recall the previous surah which began with **وَٱلصَّٰفَّٰتِ**, mentioning the angels arranged in rows. This connection becomes significant as we continue through the surah, where angels appear again in the narrative.

The ayah then presents an oath: **وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ذِى ٱلذِّكْرِ** - "By the Quran containing reminder." The word **ذِى** (possessor of) carries beautiful depth. The Quran is not merely called **ٱلذِّكْرِ** (the reminder) as an external label. Rather, it is described as the possessor of reminder, filled with it, containing it within itself. Consider how a person's name might be Kareem (noble), which describes them from the outside. But when you say someone possesses nobility, that they are filled with Karama, you're speaking about what lies deep within them. Similarly, the first exposure to the Quran reveals it as reminder, but as you immerse yourself deeper, you discover how much reminder it truly possesses.

What makes this oath remarkable is what follows - or rather, what doesn't follow. There is no stated response to the oath. Allah ﷻ swears by the Quran but leaves the conclusion unstated. This is a unique rhetorical feature that appears multiple times in the Quran. Why? Because Allah ﷻ expects those who reflect to reach the conclusion themselves. The evidence has been placed before them - the Quran itself, filled with reminder. The natural conclusion should be that the one who brought this Quran is indeed a messenger from Allah ﷻ. The disbelievers, however, refuse to connect these two obvious truths.

translation: But those who disbelieve are in pride and dissension
commentary:
**بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ**

*But those who disbelieve are in pride and dissension.*

Instead of reaching the obvious conclusion, the disbelievers remain trapped. The word **بَلِ** (rather, but) signals a sharp turn. Rather than accepting the truth, those who disbelieve are described as being **فِى عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ** - in pride and dissension.

The word **عِزَّةٍ** (pride) describes their state of being absorbed, sunk, immersed in their sense of authority and superiority. They hold onto their pride, considering themselves too elevated, too honored to accept what has been placed before them. They are drowning in their own sense of importance.

Then comes **شِقَاقٍ** (dissension), a word rich with meaning. Its root **ش-ق-ق** conveys the idea of a crack, a split, a separation between two things that can never meet again. When the sky splits open on the Day of Judgment (**إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنشَقَّتْ**), the parts will never come together again. When rock cracks and tumbles down opposite sides of a cliff, those pieces cannot be recovered or rejoined.

Here, **شِقَاقٍ** describes the complete separation the disbelievers maintain between evidence and conclusion. The evidence stands crystal clear - the Quran with all its power and reminder. The conclusion should naturally follow - that the one who brought it is a true messenger. But their stubbornness creates an unbridgeable gap. They refuse to let these two obvious truths come together. This is why Allah ﷻ doesn't even mention the conclusion in the previous ayah. Their **شِقَاقٍ** is so severe that they will never allow themselves to reach it.

translation: How many a generation have We destroyed before them, and they [then] called out; but it was not a time for escape
commentary:
**كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ فَنَادَوا۟ وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ**

*How many a generation have We destroyed before them, and they [then] called out, but it was not a time for escape.*

The word **كَمْ** (how many) here expresses more than a simple question - it carries the weight of complaint and emphasis. It's like saying, "How much patience did I show you? How many favors did I do for you? Have you lost count?" Allah ﷻ is saying: How many generations before them did I destroy? How many times has this pattern repeated?

The word **قَرْنٍ** refers to both towns and generations, to eras and communities that came before. These were people just like the Quraysh, who responded to warnings with the same pride and dissension, who maintained the same stubborn separation between evidence and truth.

**فَنَادَوا۟** - "Then they called out." When the destruction finally came, they cried out. Notice the ayah doesn't specify to whom they called or what they said. This omission is deeply significant. In their final moments, they're just calling out desperately - to anyone, to anything. Can anyone help? Is there anyone there? The fire of their lives is burning out, and they've lost all hope that calling to specific people would matter. Would their parents still be alive? Would the authorities help? The destruction is so complete that they simply cry out into the void.

**وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ** - "But it was not a time for escape." The word **لَاتَ** is a unique construction in Arabic, considered by some scholars to be from the Yemeni dialect. It's formed by adding the letter **ت** to **لَا** (no/not). This addition transforms the meaning from simple negation to time-specific negation. It means: absolutely not at that particular moment, not at that time.

Before the destruction came, they had time. They had opportunities for refuge, for protection, for escape. The warnings had surrounded them, but Allah ﷻ held back the consequences, giving them chance after chance. But when the final destruction arrived, **لَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ** - it was absolutely not the time anymore to call for any kind of refuge or escape.

translation: And they wonder that there has come to them a warner from among themselves. And the disbelievers say, "This is a magician and a liar
commentary:
**وَعَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ**

*And they wonder that there has come to them a warner from among themselves. And the disbelievers say, "This is a magician and a liar."*

Now we see the response of the Quraysh. **وَعَجِبُوٓا۟** - they found it strange, they wondered. In the previous surah, we saw them mocking and making fun. Here we see the other side - their bewilderment. What specifically did they find strange? **أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ** - that a warner came to them from among themselves.

Why should this be strange? Think about how warnings work. When someone warns you about danger, they typically bring information from outside, from somewhere you haven't been. In ancient times, before mass media, if a town needed to hear about an approaching storm or an invading army, someone had to travel out, witness it, and return with the news. The warning comes from external observation.

But the Prophet ﷺ didn't leave and come back with news. He didn't travel to distant lands to receive his message. He was one of them - **مِّنْهُمْ** - from among themselves. His claim was even more remarkable: the warning came to him. Revelation descended upon him where he was. They couldn't see this revelation, and this baffled them. How can you warn us when you haven't gone anywhere to get this knowledge? How can you claim to have information we don't have when you've been right here with us?

But notice the shift in tone: **وَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ** - "And the disbelievers say." The wording changes from "they" to specifically "the disbelievers," emphasizing their rejection. Their response is harsh: **هَـٰذَا سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ** - "This is a magician and a liar."

**سَـٰحِرٌ** (magician) and **كَذَّابٌ** (perpetual liar) - this combination reveals the inconsistency in their accusation. The form **كَذَّابٌ** emphasizes continuous, repeated lying. But here's the contradiction: if they truly found the matter strange, that means they couldn't explain it. Finding something strange means lacking an explanation. Yet by the end of their statement, they offer a confident explanation - he's a magician and a liar.

There's another inconsistency. Magic and lying are related but distinct. However, in their society, magicians weren't viewed with the pure contempt we might expect. Magic was considered a skill, something people looked up to, even admired. But magic is fundamentally a visual art - it's meant to be seen, not heard. The Prophet ﷺ wasn't performing visual tricks. He was reciting words, delivering a message. How does one perform magic through speech alone? Their accusation doesn't hold together logically, exposing the weakness of their rejection and the depth of their **شِقَاقٍ** - their stubborn refusal to connect the obvious evidence before them with the truth it points toward.

translation: Has he made the gods [only] one God? Indeed, this is a curious thing
commentary:
أَجَعَلَ ٱلْـَٔالِهَةَ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عُجَابٌ

When their character assassinations failed to hold up under scrutiny, the disbelievers finally addressed the real issue that disturbed them: "Did he make all the gods into just one God? Indeed, this is a strange thing!" (إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عُجَابٌ).

The word عُجَاب expresses extreme strangeness and wonder. Notice how the conversation shifts. First they threw out accusations - magician, liar - but those labels contained their own contradictions. So then the truth emerged: what really bothered them was the message of Tawhid itself. The consolidation of all their deities into One God struck at the foundation of everything they knew.

This wasn't just a theological adjustment. It was asking them to accept that their ancestors were wrong, that their grandfathers didn't know what they were talking about, that generations of tradition were built on falsehood. The psychological barrier was immense. They're essentially saying to each other: "Can you believe he's telling us our forefathers were foolish? That we should abandon everything we inherited?"

The expression of this objection reveals something profound: when people run out of logical arguments, they fall back on what truly troubles them. Strip away the insults and deflections, and you find the core resistance - in this case, the unwillingness to abandon polytheism and accept the Oneness of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And the eminent among them went forth, [saying], "Continue, and be patient over [the defense of] your gods. Indeed, this is a thing intended
commentary:
وَٱنطَلَقَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ ٱمْشُوا۟ وَٱصْبِرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ يُرَادُ

The chieftains - المَلَأُ, those who filled the tribal council - now walked away from where the Prophet ﷺ was reciting Quran. But they didn't leave quietly. They used their social authority to influence others who were listening.

Picture this scene: The Prophet ﷺ is reciting, and a mixed crowd has gathered - some believers, some slaves, some curious youth, some Makkans who haven't decided yet. When one elder shows up to such a gathering, it's significant. But when all the chieftains arrive together, their presence carries tremendous weight. They listen for a while, exchange their dismissive comments, and then walk off.

But as they depart, they look back at the crowd - people over whom they have influence - and start commanding them: امْشُوا - "Move it! Get out of here! What are you still doing here?" They treated the crowd like immature children who needed to be herded away from danger. "Come on, come on, don't waste your time. Get away from him!"

Then they added: وَٱصْبِرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِكُمْ - "Have patience with your gods. Remain steadfast on your religion." The irony is rich - they're calling for "patience" while displaying none themselves. They're the ones who couldn't bear to listen.

Their final assessment: إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ يُرَادُ - "This is something with an ulterior motive, a hidden agenda." They positioned themselves as the wise elders who could see through the deception that fooled the masses. "We're experienced. We know when someone has a secret agenda. Don't be so gullible."

translation: We have not heard of this in the latest religion. This is not but a fabrication
commentary:
مَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِى ٱلْمِلَّةِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا ٱخْتِلَـٰقٌ

They continued: "We never heard anything like this in the former religion, in the old ways." The phrase فِى ٱلْمِلَّةِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ refers to their ancestral traditions.

This objection reveals a particular type of intellectual pride. Some people carry the attitude that they already know everything worth knowing. If valid information exists, they would surely possess it. Therefore, if they've never encountered something before, it must be invalid by definition. "If this was worth knowing, I would have been the first to know it. Since I didn't hear about it, it must be useless."

This is an extreme form of intellectual arrogance. It closes the door to new knowledge based solely on the premise: "I don't already own this information, therefore it cannot be true."

They dismissed the message as إِلَّا ٱخْتِلَـٰقٌ - "nothing but a fabrication." The word Ikhtilaq suggests an elaborate creative invention, something made up with no foundation. They were saying: "He went out of his way to create this elaborate story. That's why you're so captivated - it's his creativity, his fiction-making. But see through it and move on."

translation: Has the message been revealed to him out of [all of] us?" Rather, they are in doubt about My message. Rather, they have not yet tasted My punishment
commentary:
أَءُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّن ذِكْرِى ۖ بَل لَّمَّا يَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِ

The chieftains asked mockingly: "Was the Reminder revealed to him out of all of us?" They couldn't accept that from among all the leaders and elders of Quraysh - the recognized possessors of wisdom in Makkah - this revelation came to Muhammad ﷺ. "Were there no better qualified candidates available? We're the ones everyone recognizes as wise. None of us received this wisdom, but he did? Really?"

This objection exposed their wounded pride. They felt they deserved prophethood more than he did by virtue of their social status.

Allah ﷻ responds by cutting through their pretense: بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّن ذِكْرِى- "Rather, they are in doubt about My Reminder." Their real problem isn't about who received the message. They're fundamentally uncertain about whether remembering Allah ﷻ is even necessary.

Then comes a profound observation: بَل لَّمَّا يَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِ- "Rather, they have not yet tasted punishment." The word لَمَّا means "not yet," indicating something that hasn't happened but will.

Some people reach a point where only punishment can straighten them out. They've exhausted all reasoning, ignored all signs, dismissed all warnings. The only thing left that can wake them up is to face consequences. These Quraysh leaders had reached that stage. They were so deluded in their arrogance that until punishment touched them directly, they would continue in their rejection.

translation: Or do they have the depositories of the mercy of your Lord, the Exalted in Might, the Bestower
commentary:
أَمْ عِندَهُمْ خَزَآئِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْوَهَّابِ

Allah ﷻ now poses rhetorical questions that expose the absurdity of their position: "Or do they possess the treasures of your Lord's mercy?"

The disbelievers acted as though revelation required their approval. They behaved as if they controlled access to divine wisdom - as though nothing could happen in Makkah without their permission. When the Prophet ﷺ received revelation, they responded as an employer might when an employee rearranges the store without authorization: "Why did you do that? I didn't tell you to. This is my store."

Their attitude was: "This is our town. What do you mean wisdom came to you? What do you mean you're making public announcements to crowds? Who sanctioned this? Who gave you permission?"

But the treasures of mercy belong to رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْوَهَّابِ- your Lord, the Mighty, the Bestower. Al-'Azeez is the One whose authority cannot be challenged. Al-Wahhab is the One who gives gifts as He ﷻ wills, not as they demand.

The question devastates their pretense: Do you own the vaults of divine mercy? Can you dictate who receives revelation? The implied answer exposes their arrogance for what it is - a delusion of control they never possessed.

translation: Or is theirs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and what is between them? Then let them ascend through [any] ways of access
commentary:
أَمْ لَهُم مُّلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَلْيَرْتَقُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبِ

The second rhetorical question escalates: "Or do they possess the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them?"

These leaders controlled a small patch of land around Makkah. They had influence over some tribes and trade routes. But they carried themselves as though they owned the cosmos. This tiny sphere of worldly authority inflated their egos to the point where they presumed to question Allah's ﷻ choice of messenger.

فَلْيَرْتَقُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبِ - "Then let them ascend through the means." The word أَسْبَاب  can refer to ropes, ladders, or any means of climbing. The challenge is clear: if you think you have authority over the heavens and earth, then climb up there. Ascend level upon level into the sky. Go take your seat of authority in the celestial realm.

The absurdity of their pretense is laid bare. You can't even reach the first heaven, yet you question the Lord of all the heavens about His ﷻ choice? You have no dominion beyond your small patch of desert, yet you act as gatekeepers of divine revelation?

translation: [They are but] soldiers [who will be] defeated there among the companies [of disbelievers]
commentary:
جُندٌ مَّا هُنَالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ

Allah ﷻ declares about these Quraysh leaders: "Just another army there, bound to be defeated, from among the allied groups."

The phrase جُندٌ مَّا - "just any army" - is powerfully dismissive. These people who strutted with such arrogance, who thought themselves the arbiters of truth, who presumed authority over revelation itself - they're just another army. Nothing special. Nothing unprecedented.

مَهْزُومٌ- they are destined for defeat, for being routed and destroyed. And they're not even unique in their opposition: مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ- they're just like the groups and coalitions that came before them.

This sets up the historical parallels that follow. Before the Quraysh convinced themselves of their exceptionalism, other powerful nations and leaders made the same mistake. Each thought themselves too mighty to fall. Each rejected their messenger with similar arrogance. And each met the same end.

translation: The people of Noah denied before them, and [the tribe of] 'Aad and Pharaoh, the owner of stakes
commentary:
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ ذُو ٱلْأَوْتَادِ

Before these Quraysh, the people of Nuh (AS) denied. So did the 'Ad. And Fir'awn (Pharaoh), the possessor of stakes.

Each of these represents a different form of arrogance and power. The people of Nuh (AS) had longevity and dismissed the message for generations. The 'Ad had physical strength and considered themselves invincible. But Fir'awn receives a unique description: ذُو ٱلْأَوْتَادِ - possessor of stakes or pegs.

What does this mean? When ancient armies established bases or went on campaigns, they set up tents. Even today, military forces use tents for field operations - for supplies, barracks, temporary command centers. Tents allow armies to stay mobile, to move quickly and establish presence anywhere.

By describing Fir'awn as the "possessor of pegs," Allah ﷻ captures the essence of his military power. His armies were spread everywhere, constantly on the move, maintaining control through mobilized force. He had the resources to establish military presence wherever needed, to expand his empire or crush rebellions. The tent pegs represent a vast, organized, mobile military machine.

Yet despite this overwhelming worldly power - despite the infrastructure, the armies, the reach of his authority - Fir'awn fell. His stakes didn't save him. His military might meant nothing when Allah's ﷻ decree came.

translation: And [the tribe of] Thamud and the people of Lot and the companions of the thicket. Those are the companies
commentary:
وَثَمُودُ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ لْـَٔيْكَةِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ

The list continues: the Thamud, the people of Lut (AS), and the companions of the thicket (the people of Shu'ayb AS). أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ - "Those are the groups."

This phrase connects back to ayah 11, which referred to the Quraysh as just another defeated group "from among the ahzab." Now we understand: these are the ahzab. These powerful nations, these confident rejecters - they formed the precedent. The Quraysh are walking the same path, joining the same category of historical failures.

The variety in the list is significant. Different peoples, different times, different forms of transgression - but united in their rejection of messengers and their ultimate destruction. The message to the Quraysh is clear: your opposition isn't novel, and neither will be your fate.

translation: Each of them denied the messengers, so My penalty was justified
commentary:
إِن كُلٌّ إِلَّا كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ عِقَابِ

What united all these diverse nations? إِن كُلٌّ إِلَّا كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ  - "Not one but denied the messengers."

Notice the plural: الرُّسُلَ  - the messengers. Even though each nation had its specific messenger, their rejection of one was a rejection of the message carried by all. To reject Nuh (AS) was to reject the same truth that Musa (AS) would later bring. To deny Lut (AS) was to deny what every prophet taught. The message of Tawhid and submission to Allah ﷻ is one, though the messengers were many.

فَحَقَّ عِقَابِ  - "So My punishment was justified." The word حَقَّ means something became due, became deserved, became a right. After messengers came with clear signs, after warnings were delivered, after every opportunity was given - punishment became the just consequence. It was earned. It was deserved.

This establishes a pattern and a warning. The Quraysh are treading the exact path these nations traveled. They're repeating the same mistakes, displaying the same arrogance, making the same denials. And the trajectory is clear: they're heading toward the same end.

translation: And these [disbelievers] await not but one blast [of the Horn]; for it there will be no delay
commentary:
وَمَا يَنظُرُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً مَّا لَهَا مِن فَوَاقٍ

As for these current rejecters in Makkah: وَمَا يَنظُرُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً - "These people await nothing but a single blast."

The word يَنظُرُ means to wait for, to look forward to - though they don't realize what they're waiting for. While they mock and reject, while they scheme and plot, what actually awaits them is صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً - a single cry, a single shout, a single blast.

Then comes the terrifying description: مَّا لَهَا مِن فَوَاقٍ - "from which there is no recovery." The word فَوَاق refers to the brief pause between two milkings of a camel, a moment of rest or recovery. The ayah says there will be no such pause, no recovery period, no chance to regroup.

When that blast comes, it's over. Instantaneous. Final. No time to reconsider, no opportunity to ask for another chance, no recovery phase. The punishment that previous nations experienced stretched over time - drowning, earthquakes, storms. But this final judgment will arrive as a single, devastating moment from which there is no return.

translation: And they say, "Our Lord, hasten for us our share [of the punishment] before the Day of Account
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا عَجِّل لَّنَا قِطَّنَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ

In their arrogance and mockery, these disbelievers made a sarcastic request: "Our Lord, hasten for us our portion before the Day of Account."

The word قِطَّ refers to a share or portion, specifically an account or record. In pre-Islamic commerce, when people bought on credit, merchants would keep a register - a running tab of debts. At the end of the month, they would settle accounts. The British call it "putting it on your tab." Hotels do the same: "Just charge it to room 105." At checkout, you receive an invoice showing everything you owe. That final accounting, that comprehensive record - that's the qitta.

The disbelievers are saying mockingly: "If this Day of Judgment is going to give us a full audit of our deeds, Master - since You control everything, right? - why don't you just send us the report now? Why wait? Let's see how we're doing so far. Give us our qitta right now, before يَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ , before the Day of Full Reckoning."

It's dripping with sarcasm. They don't believe the Day is coming, so they're calling the bluff, so to speak. "Go ahead, show us this punishment you keep warning about. Let's see our record. We're ready."

What they don't understand is that this mocking request will be answered - but not in the way they imagine. When that single blast comes that has no recovery, when the Day they're joking about actually arrives, they'll receive their qitta. And they'll wish they had never asked for it.

translation: Be patient over what they say and remember Our servant, David, the possessor of strength; indeed, he was one who repeatedly turned back [to Allah]
commentary:
ٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا دَاوُۥدَ ذَا ٱلْأَيْدِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ

Allah ﷻ employs a powerful psychological strategy in this ayah. When someone is upset or disturbed, the best remedy is often to redirect their attention entirely. This is what mothers instinctively do with their children. When a child is upset about something, a wise mother simply changes the subject to something completely different. The mind shifts, and the agitation dissipates.

This is precisely what Allah ﷻ does for His Messenger ﷺ here. The Prophet ﷺ was being subjected to constant ridicule and rejection from the Quraysh. Their words were hurtful and their mockery was relentless. So Allah ﷻ tells him: اصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ (Be patient with what they say). But He ﷻ doesn't stop there. He immediately redirects the Prophet's ﷺ thoughts: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا دَاوُۥدَ (And remember Our servant Dawud).

Notice the beautiful transition. Don't dwell on their words. Instead, think about Prophet Dawud (AS). Think about the gifts I gave him. When the Prophet ﷺ reflects on Allah's ﷻ gifts to Dawud (AS), it naturally leads him to reflect on Allah's ﷻ gifts to him. This mental shift completely removes his mind from the harmful words of his opponents.

Allah ﷻ describes Dawud (AS) as ذَا ٱلْأَيْدِ (possessor of many hands). The word أَيْد refers to strength and capability, but it also carries the meaning of having many helping hands. Dawud (AS) had tremendous support, resources and power at his disposal. Yet despite all this worldly power and authority, إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (he was constantly turning back to Allah). The word أَوَّاب comes from آب which means to return. Someone who is أَوَّاب is someone who repeatedly returns to Allah ﷻ, over and over again. No matter how blessed Dawud (AS) was with worldly power, his heart remained connected to his Master.

translation: Indeed, We subjected the mountains [to praise] with him, exalting [Allah] in the [late] afternoon and [after] sunrise
commentary:
إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا ٱلْجِبَالَ مَعَهُۥ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِشْرَاقِ

Allah ﷻ now describes the extraordinary blessings He granted to Dawud (AS). إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا ٱلْجِبَالَ مَعَهُۥ (We subdued the mountains along with him). The mountains themselves were made subservient to him, يُسَبِّحْنَ (declaring Allah's ﷻ perfection) بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِشْرَاقِ (in the late afternoon to evening hours and in the bright morning hours).

The word عَشِيّ refers to that time period from late afternoon through evening, while إِشْرَاق describes the later morning when the sun is bright and radiant. During these specific times, the mountains would join Dawud (AS) in glorifying Allah ﷻ. This wasn't just a silent participation – these massive, solid structures would audibly declare the perfection of their Creator alongside this blessed prophet.

translation: And the birds were assembled, all with him repeating [praises]
commentary:
وَٱلطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ

The miracle extended beyond mountains. وَٱلطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً (And the birds, gathered together). The word مَحْشُورَة describes birds being herded or gathered in massive flocks. If you've ever witnessed an evening commute of birds, you've seen endless streams of them flying overhead, wave after wave, where you cannot tell where the flock begins or ends. 

Different species of birds, in their entire flocks, would be herded toward Dawud (AS). They would come to him in these massive gatherings. كُلٌّ لَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (Each one constantly returning to him). Every bird, from every flock, would keep coming back, joining him in his praise of Allah ﷻ. The same quality that defined Dawud (AS) – being أَوَّاب, constantly returning to Allah ﷻ – was shared by these birds as they continuously returned to join him in glorification.

translation: And We strengthened his kingdom and gave him wisdom and discernment in speech
commentary:
وَشَدَدْنَا مُلْكَهُۥ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ ٱلْخِطَابِ

Beyond these natural miracles, Allah ﷻ granted Dawud (AS) three crucial elements for leadership. وَشَدَدْنَا مُلْكَهُۥ (We fortified his kingdom). His dominion was strengthened and secured. This wasn't just about military might, but about the stability and integrity of his entire rule.

وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ (And We gave him wisdom). Wisdom here refers to deep understanding, sound judgment and the ability to see through complex situations to their core truth.

وَفَصْلَ ٱلْخِطَابِ (And decisive speech). This phrase is particularly significant. The word فَصْل means to separate, to cut through, to make decisive. In Arabic culture, there's a saying: لِكُلِّ كَلَامٍ جَوَابٌ (Every speech has a counter-speech). For every statement, there's a rebuttal. For every argument, there's a counterargument. This is the nature of discourse and debate.

But when someone possesses فَصْل ٱلْخِطَابِ, their speech is so clear, so decisive, so obviously correct that no one can argue against it. When Dawud (AS) would pass a verdict, everyone would simply accept it. His judgments were final. There was no room for debate or dispute. People would leave his court satisfied, knowing justice had been served. Even those who lost their cases would accept the decision without protest. This is the power of فَصْل ٱلْخِطَابِ – speech that settles matters completely.

translation: And has there come to you the news of the adversaries, when they climbed over the wall of [his] prayer chamber
commentary:
۞ وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَصْمِ إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا۟ ٱلْمِحْرَابَ

Now Allah ﷻ transitions to a remarkable story. وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَصْمِ (Has the news come to you of the two disputing parties?). The word خَصْم is actually singular in form but plural in meaning, referring to opposing groups engaged in a dispute.

إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا۟ ٱلْمِحْرَابَ (When they scaled the wall of the sanctuary). To understand the gravity of this, we need to understand what تَسَوَّرُوا means. The root سُور gives us the word سُورَة, which we use for chapters of the Quran. But سُور originally refers to the massive outer walls of a fortified city or castle – those towering barriers that were designed to be completely impregnable.

These walls were impossibly high and thick, meant to protect the ruler from any external threat. The verb تَسَوَّرُوا means to scale or climb over these walls – something that should be absolutely impossible. Yet here, multiple people managed to do exactly that.

The word مِحْرَاب refers to the special prayer chamber, but it also denotes the most secure, palatial room in the highest quarters of the palace. Picture this: Dawud (AS) is in his private chambers, many floors up in the castle. The only access should be through a winding staircase, with guards stationed at every level and locked doors separating each floor. He is in complete security and privacy.

But suddenly, from the window of his high chamber, armed men begin climbing in. One after another, they scale the impossible wall and enter through his window. The plural verb form تَسَوَّرُوا indicates they kept coming, one after another. This wasn't just one or two people – it was a continuous stream of warriors entering his most private, most secure space.

translation: When they entered upon David and he was alarmed by them? They said, "Fear not. [We are] two adversaries, one of whom has wronged the other, so judge between us with truth and do not exceed [it] and guide us to the sound path
commentary:
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ دَاوُۥدَ فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ خَصْمَانِ بَغَىٰ بَعْضُنَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تُشْطِطْ وَٱهْدِنَآ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ

إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ دَاوُۥدَ (When they entered upon Dawud). Think about what this scene implies. Why would someone scale an impossible wall to enter the king's private quarters through a window? The obvious assumption is assassination. These weren't ordinary visitors – these were clearly skilled warriors, and a large group of them at that.

فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ (He was startled by them). The word فَزِعَ describes a particular kind of fear – not necessarily terror, but being startled, shaken, thrown off balance. It's the kind of fear that prevents clear thinking. This is crucial to understand. Yes, Dawud (AS) was powerful. Yes, he was the one who defeated Jalut (Goliath). Yes, Allah ﷻ had given him the ability to soften iron like putty in his hands. He could defend himself against one attacker, even several. But thirty or forty trained warriors flooding into his chamber all at once? That's a different matter entirely. He was rattled, and understandably so.

Immediately, they spoke in unison: قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ (They said: Do not fear). The fact that they had to say this confirms how threatening their entrance was. Their words continue: خَصْمَانِ (We are two disputing parties) بَغَىٰ بَعْضُنَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ (one of us has transgressed against the other).

Notice what they're saying: "We're not here for you. We have a problem with each other." But then comes the implicit threat in their request: فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ (So judge between us with truth). They're essentially demanding justice from a position of power. They've just demonstrated their capability by doing the impossible – scaling his walls and entering his private quarters. Now they're telling him to judge fairly.

وَلَا تُشْطِطْ (And do not be unjust). The word تُشْطِطْ carries multiple meanings. One interpretation is "don't put us off" – don't tell us to come back next week, don't delay our case. We came here because we couldn't get a proper hearing through normal channels. Another meaning is "don't rush through this" – give our case proper attention and thought. It can also mean "don't be unfair in your judgment." 

All of these meanings create pressure on the judge. They're not just asking for justice – they're demanding it, and the implicit threat is clear in the circumstances of their arrival.

وَٱهْدِنَآ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ (Guide us to the most even path). Whatever you decide, they say, we will accept. But you better make sure your judgment is sound. The pressure on Dawud (AS) is immense. He's been startled, he's surrounded, and he's being asked to judge quickly and fairly under duress.

translation: Indeed this, my brother, has ninety-nine ewes, and I have one ewe; so he said, 'Entrust her to me,' and he overpowered me in speech
commentary:
إِنَّ هَـٰذَآ أَخِى لَهُۥ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً وَلِىَ نَعْجَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ فَقَالَ أَكْفِلْنِيهَا وَعَزَّنِى فِى ٱلْخِطَابِ

Each side has a representative who presents the case. One steps forward and begins: إِنَّ هَـٰذَآ أَخِى (This is my brother). Immediately, we understand this is a family dispute, which makes it even more bitter. He continues: لَهُۥ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً (He has ninety-nine sheep).

So who is speaking – the rich shepherd or the poor one? Clearly, the poor one. He's establishing the massive disparity between them. وَلِىَ نَعْجَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ (And I have a single sheep). 

The word نَعْجَة by itself is enough to say "I have a sheep." But he adds وَٰحِدَةٌ (one) for emphasis. "I only have one. Just one." He's making his case skillfully, emphasizing the injustice of the situation. He's a skilled orator presenting his grievance.

فَقَالَ أَكْفِلْنِيهَا (And he said: Entrust it to me). The word أَكْفِلْنِيهَا means "let me take care of it" or "give it to me to care for." The poor shepherd is explaining that his brother first approached him gently: "Look, I have experience managing ninety-nine sheep. I could take care of yours too. Why don't you let me manage it for you?" And the poor shepherd refused. "No, I have my own sheep. I'll take care of it myself."

وَعَزَّنِى فِى ٱلْخِطَابِ (And he overpowered me in speech). When gentle persuasion didn't work, the rich brother became more forceful and authoritative. "Just give me your sheep." The word عَزَّنِى implies dominance, pressure, even bullying through speech. He's the older brother, perhaps, or has more social standing. He began to impose his will through increasingly aggressive demands.

This is why the case escalated. The poor brother gathered his supporters, the rich brother gathered his supporters, tensions rose, they tried to get a court date through official channels but couldn't get one in time, so they took this dramatic action – scaling the wall to get an immediate hearing from the highest judge in the land.

translation: [David] said, "He has certainly wronged you in demanding your ewe [in addition] to his ewes. And indeed, many associates oppress one another, except for those who believe and do righteous deeds - and few are they." And David became certain that We had tried him, and he asked forgiveness of his Lord and fell down bowing [in prostration] and turned in repentance [to Allah]
commentary:
قَالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤَالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلَىٰ نِعَاجِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْخُلَطَآءِ لَيَبْغِى بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَقَلِيلٌ مَّا هُمْ ۗ وَظَنَّ دَاوُۥدُ أَنَّمَا فَتَنَّـٰهُ فَٱسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُۥ وَخَرَّ رَاكِعًا وَأَنَابَ ۩

Dawud (AS) immediately responds: قَالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤَالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلَىٰ نِعَاجِهِۦ (He has certainly wronged you by demanding your sheep to add to his own sheep). The verdict is instant and decisive. The poor shepherd wins. The rich shepherd, who hasn't even presented his side, has already been declared wrong.

Here we must pause and recognize something critical. Remember what we learned about Dawud (AS) just moments ago? He possesses فَصْل ٱلْخِطَابِ – decisive speech, the ability to judge so clearly that everyone accepts his verdict. Yet here, there's a glaring problem.

He didn't hear both sides. The most basic principle of justice is to hear all parties before rendering judgment. But Dawud (AS), still shaken from the startling entrance (فَفَزِعَ), wanting these armed men out of his private quarters as quickly as possible, rushed to judgment based solely on the first presentation.

This echoes a lesson from the story of Musa (AS). In Surah Al-Qasas, two men were fighting – one from the Israelites and one from the Egyptians. فَاسْتَغَاثَهُ الَّذِي مِن شِيعَتِهِ عَلَى الَّذِي مِنْ عَدُوِّهِ (The one from his own people called for his help against his enemy). Musa (AS) immediately sided with the Israelite, only to discover later that the Israelite was actually the aggressor. It was a rush to judgment based on assumption rather than investigation. Dawud (AS) has made a similar error here – assuming the rich brother must be the oppressor simply because he's wealthy.

But Allah ﷻ doesn't leave the ayah there. He adds a profound universal principle: وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْخُلَطَآءِ لَيَبْغِى بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ (And indeed, many partners transgress against one another). The word خُلَطَاء refers to people who are mixed together, intermingled – business partners, relatives living together, siblings sharing a room, coworkers in close quarters. When people interact frequently and intimately, they inevitably step on each other's toes. They get on each other's nerves. Conflicts arise naturally from proximity.

Think about when your brother or sister moves away for college. You miss them, you love them, you speak fondly of them. Then they come home. Everyone cries, you embrace, you're so happy to see them. Three hours later, you're fighting: "What did you do with my room?" "Well, you weren't here!" People who are mixed together in daily life will naturally have conflicts. This is human nature.

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (Except those who believe and do righteous deeds). The exception is those whose faith and good character enable them to control themselves, to forgive, to maintain patience even in close quarters. وَقَلِيلٌ مَّا هُمْ (And few they are). Allah ﷻ acknowledges how rare this quality is. How few people can live in close proximity without conflict. This is the reality of human relationships.

Then comes the turning point: وَظَنَّ دَاوُۥدُ أَنَّمَا فَتَنَّـٰهُ (And Dawud realized that We had tested him). The moment he passed his verdict, something happened. In some narrations, the men disappeared – they simply vanished. Whether they disappeared or not, Dawud (AS) suddenly realized: this was a test. This was Allah ﷻ teaching him something.

The test wasn't about personal sin. The classical interpretations that suggest this story relates to Dawud (AS) desiring someone's wife are problematic and find no support in the clear language of the Quran itself. What the Quran explicitly tells us is much simpler and more powerful: a judge was placed under extreme pressure, his emotional state was compromised by fear and startlement (فَفَزِعَ), and in that compromised state, he made a rushed judgment without hearing both sides. He was tested in his capacity as a judge, and he realized his error.

The profound lesson here relates directly to the beginning of the passage. Remember, this story is being told to console the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who was under tremendous pressure from the Quraysh. When a person is under pressure, their judgment becomes impaired. They cannot think clearly. They make mistakes even when they're trying to do the right thing.

فَٱسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُۥ (So he sought forgiveness from his Master). Immediately upon realizing his error, Dawud (AS) turned to Allah ﷻ in repentance. وَخَرَّ رَاكِعًا (And he fell down in prostration). The word خَرَّ means to fall, and رَاكِعًا can mean bowing but also implies falling to one's knees in humility and submission, pressing down with one's hands.

وَأَنَابَ (And turned in repentance). He made sincere tawbah. This is the quality of أَوَّاب that was mentioned at the beginning – constantly returning to Allah ﷻ. Even the greatest of prophets, when they recognize an error, immediately return to their Lord. They don't justify, they don't make excuses, they don't delay. They turn back to Allah ﷻ instantly.

This is the complete picture the Quran paints: a story of human limitation, divine testing, immediate recognition of error and sincere repentance. It's a story that teaches judges to maintain proper emotional state and due process, even under pressure. And ultimately, it's a story that consoles the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ by reminding him that even the greatest prophets faced tests, made human errors in judgment and found peace only in returning to Allah ﷻ.

translation: So We forgave him that; and indeed, for him is nearness to Us and a good place of return
commentary:
فَغَفَرْنَا لَهُۥ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَهُۥ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ

"So We forgave him for that, and indeed, he has nearness to Us and a good place of return."

Allah ﷻ declares His forgiveness for Prophet Dawood (AS) regarding that rush to judgment described in the previous passage. The word فَغَفَرْنَا - "We forgave" - shows Allah's mercy immediately following the lesson. This forgiveness itself becomes a teaching point: even those closest to Allah ﷻ can make errors in judgment, but what distinguishes them is their immediate return to Him.

The ayah continues with a powerful reassurance: وَإِنَّ لَهُۥ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ - "and indeed, he has nearness to Us." The word زُلْفَىٰ refers to a high station and honor with Allah ﷻ. This directly answers a question left in the minds of readers from the previous surah, where people of wealth and power were told that their money and children don't grant them status with Allah ﷻ. If material blessings don't give you zulfa, then what does? This story provides the answer: sincere servitude to Allah ﷻ, accountability to Him, and the ability to recognize and correct one's mistakes - these are what grant true status with the Creator.

The phrase حُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ  means "the most beautiful place to return to." The word مَـَٔابٍ comes from the root أوب , meaning to keep coming back to something. This same root gives us the word أَوَّاب , which describes someone who constantly returns to Allah ﷻ in repentance and remembrance. The message is clear: Dawood's (AS) palace awaits him in Paradise as a reward for his character and devotion.

translation: [We said], "O David, indeed We have made you a successor upon the earth, so judge between the people in truth and do not follow [your own] desire, as it will lead you astray from the way of Allah." Indeed, those who go astray from the way of Allah will have a severe punishment for having forgotten the Day of Account
commentary:
يَـٰدَاوُۥدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ خَلِيفَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ ٱلْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا نَسُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْحِسَابِ

"O Dawood, indeed We have made you a successor upon the earth, so judge between the people in truth and do not follow desire, as it will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed, those who go astray from the way of Allah will have a severe punishment for having forgotten the Day of Account."

Allah ﷻ addresses Dawood (AS) directly: يَـٰدَاوُۥدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ خَلِيفَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "O Dawood, We have made you a khalifah in the land." The word خَلِيفَةً  carries multiple meanings. It refers to someone who leaves a legacy behind, someone who will have successors following him. It also means someone given tremendous responsibility and authority in the land. This isn't just about kingship - it's about stewardship and accountability before Allah ﷻ.

The command that follows is central: فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَقِّ - "judge between people with truth." Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't say "judge yourself fairly" or "be fair in your own matters." The focus remains on his role as a judge for others. This is significant because it shows that Allah ﷻ is giving him advice not as someone who fell into personal error, but as someone with the weighty responsibility of judging between people. The standard is الْحَقِّ  - truth, justice, what is based on evidence and reality.

Then comes the warning: وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ ٱلْهَوَىٰ - "and do not follow desire." The word الْهَوَىٰ  refers to whims, gut feelings, personal inclinations. Allah ﷻ warns that following hawa will lead one astray from His path. The path of Allah ﷻ isn't what merely "feels right" - it's what is grounded in haqq, in evidence, in hearing all sides, in examining all the facts. Many times, our first assumption can be completely wrong. A judge cannot afford to make decisions based on incomplete information or personal feelings.

The ayah concludes with a sobering warning about those who stray from Allah's path: لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا نَسُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْحِسَابِ - "they will have severe punishment because of what they forgot - the Day of Account." The verb يَضِلُّونَ  suggests people who were already on the right path but then veered off course. There was no reason for them to deviate; they simply lost their way. The word نَسُوا۟  doesn't just mean to forget - it means to overlook, to neglect, to dismiss something from consideration. When people forget the Day of Judgment, they forget accountability, and judgment becomes corrupted.

translation: And We did not create the heaven and the earth and that between them aimlessly. That is the assumption of those who disbelieve, so woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire
commentary:
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَـٰطِلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ

"And We did not create the heaven and earth and that between them aimlessly. That is the assumption of those who disbelieve, so woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire."

Allah ﷻ makes a powerful philosophical statement: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَـٰطِلًا - "We did not create the heavens and the earth and what is between them without purpose." The word بَـٰطِلًا  is the opposite of haqq. If judgments should be made with haqq, it's because the universe itself was not created with batil - with pointlessness or falsehood.

This concept runs deep in the Quran's worldview. The Quran teaches us that the skies have been created with purpose, and because we're constantly exposed to the sky, we're constantly reminded that we too have a purpose. If someone claims they don't know their purpose in life, they need only look up. The skies themselves testify to purposeful creation. And what is that purpose moving toward? The skies and earth are inching toward their final goal - the Day when they will be transformed for Judgment. Allah ﷻ will give them permission to fulfill their ultimate purpose, as He says elsewhere: "when the earth is flattened" - this is what it has been waiting for all along.

The ayah identifies who denies this reality: ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ - "That is the assumption of those who disbelieve." To think creation is purposeless is the mindset of those who reject faith. And the consequence? فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ - "Woe to those who disbelieve from the Fire." Wail represents the worst form of punishment imaginable, and its very substance is fire.

translation: Or should we treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like corrupters in the land? Or should We treat those who fear Allah like the wicked
commentary:
أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ كَٱلْفُجَّارِ

"Or should We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like corrupters in the land? Or should We treat those who fear Allah like the wicked?"

This ayah poses two rhetorical questions that strike at the heart of justice and accountability. أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - "Should We make those who believe and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption in the land?" The answer is obviously no, but Allah ﷻ frames it as a question to make us think deeply about the implications.

Consider what this means: there are people on earth who live with restraint. They hold themselves back from temptation. They say, "I cannot eat this because it is haram. I cannot look there because it is haram. I cannot go there or do that because of Allah's boundaries. I have specific times for prayer that I must observe. I cannot pursue just any career - there are certain codes and guidelines I must follow." This is the life of those who believe and do righteous deeds.

Now compare this to those described as مُفْسِدِينَ  - corrupters. These are people who say, "Take whatever you can. Seize whatever opportunity presents itself. If you can get away with something, why not?" Notice that most crimes occur during riots and chaos - when people think they won't be caught, when there's no immediate accountability. Even people who aren't typically criminals can resort to terrible acts when they believe there are no consequences.

The second question follows: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ كَٱلْفُجَّارِ - "Should We treat those with taqwa like the wicked?" The word الْفُجَّارِ  comes from fajr, which literally means "explosion" - like the explosion of light at dawn. A fajir is someone who explodes into sin, who has no restraints, no brakes whatsoever. They do whatever they desire whenever they desire it.

Without the concept of the afterlife and accountability, these two groups would be the same. There would be no difference between someone who restrains himself and someone who indulges every desire. The very existence of the Day of Judgment is what distinguishes the criminal from the righteous. It's what gives purpose to our restraint, to our worship, to our submission to Allah's commands. Otherwise, why pray? Why fast? Why hold ourselves back from what everyone else is freely enjoying?

This ayah establishes that Allah's justice demands accountability. The righteous and the wicked cannot be equal, and it would be fundamentally unfair - impossible for a just Creator - to treat them the same.

translation: [This is] a blessed Book which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], that they might reflect upon its verses and that those of understanding would be reminded
commentary:
كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَـٰرَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

"[This is] a blessed Book which We have revealed to you, [O Muhammad], that they might reflect upon its verses and that those of understanding would be reminded."

Allah ﷻ describes the Quran: كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَـٰرَكٌ - "A Book We have sent down to you, blessed." The word مُبَـٰرَكٌ , often translated as "blessed," carries profound meanings that deserve deeper exploration.

One dimension of barakah is that it brings out more good than expected. When you stick to this book, more goodness will come to you than you could have imagined. It has an inherent power to increase blessing in your life. But there's another beautiful meaning of barakah: the gradual extraction of good from something that has potential within it. Think of a scout who sees a young athlete and recognizes raw talent: "You have potential, but you need refinement. With proper training, you could be amazing." The talent already exists - it just needs to be brought out.

Similarly, human beings have goodness within them like a seed has potential within it. But that potential needs the right conditions to emerge. When Allah ﷻ describes water as مُبَـٰرَكٌ in the Quran, He's not just saying it's blessed - He's saying it brings out the potential in the seed. The seed always had that potential, but without water, it could never emerge and mature.

This is why the Quran is mubarakun. It's like that blessed water on the seed of human goodness. It takes the goodness within us and brings it out, develops it, and brings it to full maturity. Allah ﷻ created the human being فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ - in the best possible form. But you won't be impressed by the seed - you'll be impressed by the tree it becomes. The Quran elsewhere gives us the parable of the believer as a good tree whose roots are deeply planted and whose branches reach into the sky. This is the result of the Quran's barakah working on the human soul.

But how does this transformation happen? لِّيَدَّبَّرُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ - "so that they might reflect deeply upon its ayat." The word تَدَبُّر  means deep, careful contemplation. It means immersing yourself in the world of Allah's words, thinking about them carefully, turning them over in your mind and heart. Reflection on the Quran is what brings out the ultimate good from a human being. This isn't casual reading - it's engaging with the text, questioning it, pondering it, letting it work on your heart and mind.

The ayah concludes: وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ - "and so that those of understanding might take heed." The أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ  are people of sound minds, people who think clearly and deeply. The Quran calls them to remember, to make an effort to retain and apply what they learn. This remembrance isn't passive - it's active engagement with divine guidance.

translation: And to David We gave Solomon. An excellent servant, indeed he was one repeatedly turning back [to Allah]
commentary:
وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُۥدَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ

"And to Dawood We gave Sulaiman. An excellent servant! Indeed, he was one who repeatedly turned back [to Allah]."

The narrative shifts as Allah ﷻ declares: وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُۥدَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ - "And to Dawood We granted Sulaiman." Every father is granted children, so what makes this phrasing special? When Allah ﷻ says He "granted" Sulaiman (AS) to Dawood (AS), it indicates something unique about this child. This son is not like other sons. There's something exceptional about him that makes him truly a gift from Allah ﷻ.

Allah ﷻ immediately highlights what makes Sulaiman (AS) special: نِعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ - "What an excellent servant!" This is the first quality Allah ﷻ chooses to emphasize. Not his wisdom, not his kingdom, not his power over the wind and jinn - but his quality as a slave of Allah ﷻ. نِعْمَ  expresses the ultimate praise: he was an awesome, exemplary slave of Allah ﷻ.

Then comes the defining characteristic: إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ - "Indeed, he was one who constantly returned to Allah." The word أَوَّابٌ  comes from the same root we saw earlier with Dawood (AS). It describes someone who keeps coming back to Allah ﷻ again and again - in remembrance, in repentance, in devotion. Just as the father was described as awwab, now the son carries the same quality. This is what made Dawood (AS) truly proud - not that his son would inherit his throne, but that his son would continue his legacy of devotion to Allah ﷻ.

This is the real gift from Allah ﷻ: to leave behind a righteous son or daughter who will carry forward your commitment to Him. This is what gives a parent true joy and what constitutes lasting success.

translation: [Mention] when there were exhibited before him in the afternoon the poised [standing] racehorses
commentary:
إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ ٱلصَّـٰفِنَـٰتُ ٱلْجِيَادُ

"[Mention] when there were exhibited before him in the afternoon the poised [standing] racehorses."

Allah ﷻ now presents a scene from Sulaiman's (AS) life: إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ - "When there was presented before him in the afternoon." The time frame is بِٱلْعَشِىِّ , which refers to the late afternoon, the hours between Dhuhr and Maghrib. During this time, something was brought before him for inspection.

What was presented? ٱلصَّـٰفِنَـٰتُ ٱلْجِيَادُ - "the elite standing horses." These aren't just any horses. The word ٱلْجِيَادُ  refers to thoroughbred horses, the finest breed of cavalry horses - the Ferrari horses of that era. These are extremely valuable animals, bred specifically for their superior qualities.

The word ٱلصَّـٰفِنَـٰتُ  describes a unique characteristic of these elite horses: they stand on three legs with one leg bent and lifted, as if on standby, ready to run at a moment's notice. This distinctive posture identifies them as premium cavalry horses, always prepared for action.

We know from other passages that Sulaiman (AS) had an extraordinary army consisting of humans, jinn, and birds. But the cavalry - the mounted forces - represented a crucial component of any ancient military force. And Sulaiman (AS) possessed an army of these elite horses. However, these particular horses weren't typically used in actual battle. They were so valuable that they were reserved for display and races. You wouldn't risk such expensive horses in combat where they could be killed.

Why would a king personally inspect horses? Kings don't normally go out to check on animals - they have people for that. But this tells us something profound about Sulaiman (AS). These horses are being presented to him because they're preparing for battle, and as their commander, he wants to personally ensure their readiness. He's not just sending his soldiers off with untested equipment - he cares deeply about his soldiers' lives, and their lives depend on these horses.

This inspection reveals Sulaiman's (AS) character as a leader. He's not going to be riding these horses himself - his soldiers will. But he takes personal responsibility for their welfare and readiness. This is part of what makes him نِعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ - an excellent servant of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And he said, "Indeed, I gave preference to the love of good [things] over the remembrance of my Lord until the sun disappeared into the curtain [of darkness]
commentary:
فَقَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ ٱلْخَيْرِ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّى حَتَّىٰ تَوَارَتْ بِٱلْحِجَابِ

"And he said, 'Indeed, I have loved the love of good things instead of the remembrance of my Lord' - until they disappeared into the veil [of sunset]."

As Sulaiman (AS) conducts his inspection, he makes a striking confession: فَقَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ ٱلْخَيْرِ - "He said, 'Indeed, I have loved the love of good.'" The word ٱلْخَيْرِ  means "good" or "good things," but for the Arabs of that time, when you mentioned "good things" in the context of military preparation, the first image that came to mind was excellent horses. These magnificent animals represented the pinnacle of valuable worldly possessions. Allah ﷻ Himself describes horses this way elsewhere in the Quran: "intense is his love for khayr" - referring to horses as representing worldly good.

Sulaiman (AS) admits: عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّى - "this took me away from the remembrance of my Lord." In becoming so absorbed in inspecting these horses - noble work, military preparation for battles in Allah's cause - he lost track of time. This wasn't frivolous distraction; this was important leadership responsibility. Yet even this caused him to miss something more important.

حَتَّىٰ تَوَارَتْ بِٱلْحِجَابِ - "Until she hid away in the veil." Who is "she"? The sun. The sun set while he was occupied with the inspection. And what does sunset mean? It means he missed Salat al-'Asr. The time for the afternoon prayer passed while he was engaged in checking on his cavalry.

This parallels an incident from the life of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ during the Battle of Ahzab (the Trench), when the Muslims, including the Prophet ﷺ himself, missed 'Asr prayer due to maintaining vigilance against the enemy. The Prophet ﷺ was deeply grieved by this and made du'a against those whose siege caused them to miss the prayer.

The profound lesson here is about priorities in service to Allah ﷻ. There are different types of service: there's the service of direct worship (Salah, fasting, Hajj), and there's the service of working for Islamic causes (da'wah, education, community building, military defense). Both are for Allah's sake, but they're not equal in rank. Salah represents the central, non-negotiable fixture in a Muslim's life - the constant connection with Allah ﷻ that cannot be displaced by any other activity, no matter how noble.

Sulaiman (AS), preparing his cavalry for battle in Allah's cause, demonstrates that even battle preparation cannot take priority over the established prayer time. This is the consciousness of an awwab - someone who constantly returns to Allah ﷻ and recognizes when even good deeds have distracted him from the best deed. This is the sensitivity of a true servant of Allah ﷻ, who immediately recognizes and acknowledges when he's let something, even something important, come between him and his primary obligation to his Lord.

translation: [He said], "Return them to me," and set about striking [their] legs and necks
commentary:
رُدُّوهَا عَلَىَّ ۖ فَطَفِقَ مَسْحًۢا بِٱلسُّوقِ وَٱلْأَعْنَاقِ

The command "Bring them back to me" has been subject to different interpretations. Some of the Israelite narrations suggest that Sulayman (AS), in his anger at missing prayer, ordered all the horses to be brought back and slaughtered them—stabbing them in their legs and necks as punishment. However, this interpretation conflicts with both the linguistic meaning of the text and the character of the prophets.

The word مَسْحًا  means to gently wipe or pour water over something, the way we make wudu by wiping over our hair. This is not a violent act. The great scholar Masharawiy held a different understanding: Sulayman (AS) felt remorse about missing the prayer, but when he ordered the horses to be brought back, it was to complete his inspection of them properly. The phrase فَطَفِقَ مَسْحًۢا بِٱلسُّوقِ وَٱلْأَعْنَاقِ describes how he passed his hand over every single leg (السُّوقِ) and neck (الْأَعْنَاقِ) of the horses, checking each one carefully. He grabbed them, assessed their strength, identified which ones were injured, and gave each horse the attention it required.

This reveals something profound: even though his inspection caused him to miss prayer—which he will seek forgiveness for—Allah ﷻ is telling us that what he did, he excelled in. He went out of his way to fulfill his responsibility toward these horses, because caring for the resources needed for jihad is also part of the religion of Allah ﷻ.

The lesson is clear: you cannot trivialize missing Salah, and you cannot trivialize the work you're doing for the religion of Allah ﷻ. Both must be taken with utmost seriousness.

translation: And We certainly tried Solomon and placed on his throne a body; then he returned
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا سُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَىٰ كُرْسِيِّهِۦ جَسَدًا ثُمَّ أَنَابَ

If the previous controversies regarding Dawud (AS) seemed significant, the interpretations surrounding this ayah take it to another level entirely. Allah ﷻ states: "We certainly tested Sulayman, and We cast upon his throne a body, then he turned back in repentance."

The word جَسَدًا  refers to a body that has no movement left in it—it may have a heartbeat, it may be breathing internally, but it's paralyzed or near death. This is similar to how the calf made by the Samiri was described as جَسَدًا—it could vibrate or make sound, but it couldn't truly move.

The ayah presents a puzzle: Allah ﷻ tested Sulayman (AS) by casting a paralyzed body on his throne. What does this mean?

Four different explanations have appeared in classical literature. Three come from Israelite sources and other narrations, while one is based directly on the Quranic text itself.

The first narration claims that Sulayman (AS) intended to have all seventy of his wives become pregnant in one night so he could produce seventy warriors for the cause of Allah ﷻ. Because he didn't say "insha'Allah," all seventy became pregnant but miscarried—except one, who gave birth to a paralyzed child. This child was then placed on his throne as a lesson.

The second version suggests that Sulayman (AS) had a brother who rebelled against him, similar to how the son of Nuh (AS) rebelled. This brother broke away from the kingdom and fought against Sulayman (AS), establishing his own realm. Eventually, Allah ﷻ paralyzed him, and his oppressed subjects killed him and brought his body back to Sulayman (AS)—his own brother's corpse.

The third narration states that Sulayman (AS) had a son and wanted to protect him from the jinn. The jinn were terrified of Sulayman's (AS) control over them and wanted to kill his son to prevent him from growing up with the same authority. To protect the child, Sulayman (AS) ordered loyal jinn to keep the boy up in the clouds and feed him there. The child died, and his body was brought back to Sulayman's (AS) throne.

These narratives appear in classical works, and some have even been attributed to scholars among the Companions. However, we must understand the context. In earlier times, our scholars accepted more material from the People of the Book without as much scrutiny as we apply today. Additionally, when something is attributed to a Companion like Ibn Abbas (RA), we must investigate carefully. He may have simply transmitted what he heard from the People of the Book without endorsing it. Furthermore, just as we scrutinize the chains of hadith transmission, the same must be done for statements attributed to the Companions. Sometimes these attributions are questionable, even if they appear in respected sources.

The interpretation that aligns most closely with the Quranic text itself is this: Allah ﷻ temporarily paralyzed Sulayman (AS) himself. Despite his control over vast armies and immense power, he was rendered motionless—cast upon his throne as a جَسَدًا, a paralyzed body. Then, when Allah ﷻ restored his health, ثُمَّ أَنَابَ—he immediately turned back to Allah ﷻ in repentance.

translation: He said, "My Lord, forgive me and grant me a kingdom such as will not belong to anyone after me. Indeed, You are the Bestower
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَهَبْ لِى مُلْكًا لَّا يَنۢبَغِى لِأَحَدٍ مِّنۢ بَعْدِىٓ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ

After his health was restored, Sulayman (AS) called out: "My Master, forgive me." He had missed Salah while occupied with the horses—even though it was for a legitimate reason, it wasn't good enough for someone of his standing with Allah ﷻ.

This highlights a crucial principle: the higher your status with Allah ﷻ, the more you're under scrutiny. For the prophets (AS), even their understandable mistakes are not taken lightly by Allah ﷻ. The things they considered sins would be accomplishments for us. They operate at a completely different level.

But notice what Sulayman (AS) says next: "And grant me a kingdom that will not be appropriate for anyone after me." It's as if he's saying to Allah ﷻ: "You tested me? I'm ready for a retest." This is like an enthusiastic student who gets a 99 on an exam and immediately asks the teacher for a harder test, confident he'll score 100 next time.

Sulayman (AS) has enthusiasm and wants to challenge himself. He wants to demonstrate even greater obedience to Allah ﷻ. He's saying: "I will never let the affairs of my kingdom distract me from my obligations again. Give me a kingdom bigger than any other—one that wouldn't even be appropriate for anyone after me because it would be too tempting for them. Give me that level of power, and I will show You that I won't be distracted again."

People had horses before. But Sulayman (AS) is asking: "Forget horses—give me armies and resources that You haven't given to anyone before, and I will not be distracted."

This is his way of turning back to Allah ﷻ—not with depression or defeat, but with zeal and readiness. إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ—"Indeed, You are the Ultimate Giver, O Allah. But I want to earn my blessings back. I want to earn my status back."

translation: So We subjected to him the wind blowing by his command, gently, wherever he directed
commentary:
فَسَخَّرْنَا لَهُ ٱلرِّيحَ تَجْرِى بِأَمْرِهِۦ رُخَآءً حَيْثُ أَصَابَ

Allah ﷻ responded to his prayer: "So We subjected to him the wind, flowing by his command, gently, wherever he directed."

In the Quran, the word رِيح (wind, singular) is often used for punishment, while رِيَاح (winds, plural) can indicate mercy. When winds blow in multiple directions—breezes coming and going calmly—they bring benefit. But when wind comes from one direction heading toward a specific target, it often brings destruction, like the رِيحٌ عَاصِفٌ (violent wind) mentioned elsewhere in the Quran.

Yet Allah ﷻ gave Sulayman (AS) control over even the most destructive winds. The phrase رُخَآءً means "softly, gently, steadily"—the opposite of violent wind. Even hurricanes would slow down and become gentle by the time they reached Sulayman (AS), then could speed up again after passing. This is power that Allah ﷻ has not granted to anyone else to this day.

Consider our modern reality: we're more technologically advanced than any civilization in human history. We can track storms via satellite, predict their paths, know exactly when a tornado will touch down and where. Yet with all this technology, can we slow down a hurricane? Can we change a tornado's course? We cannot.

حَيْثُ أَصَابَ—"Wherever he directed it." Wherever it would strike, he could slow it down and tame it. We learned about taming horses in the previous passage, and now we're learning about taming winds. This parallel exists throughout the Quran—winds and horses are both described as things that can go wild and things that can be tamed. This theme reaches its climax in Surah Al-'Adiyat.

## Ayah 37-38

وَٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ كُلَّ بَنَّآءٍ وَغَوَّاصٍ
وَءَاخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِى ٱلْأَصْفَادِ

On top of controlling the wind, Allah ﷻ gave Sulayman (AS) authority over الشَّيَـٰطِينَ—the shayateen, the devils among the jinn. These are the evil jinn, those who naturally rebel against prophets. Why would an evil jinn want to obey a prophet? They are enemies of the prophets. Yet even these devils were placed under Sulayman's (AS) command.

What work did he assign them? بَنَّآءٍ—construction work. وَغَوَّاصٍ—diving. They became his submarines. He would send them to retrieve pearls from the ocean floor, extract treasures, or perform other underwater tasks. These were the evil jinn, compelled to serve.

وَءَاخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِى ٱلْأَصْفَادِ—"And others bound together in chains." Some of these jinn were so evil they had to be kept chained, مُقَرَّنِينَ (joined together), like soldiers chained in groups. Sulayman (AS) kept them as reserves. If he needed additional forces, he could release them for specific tasks.

Think about this: if these jinn dove into the ocean with him, could they escape? Normally, yes. But Allah ﷻ had granted Sulayman (AS) such authority that they couldn't flee—they had to return. He maintained both active workers and a reserve force of chained jinn, like a weapon cache, ready to deploy when needed.

translation: And [also] the devils [of jinn] - every builder and diver
And others bound together in shackles
[We said], "This is Our gift, so grant or withhold without account
commentary:
هَـٰذَا عَطَآؤُنَا فَٱمْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

Allah ﷻ then told him: "This is Our gift. So grant freely or withhold—without account."

Consider the magnitude of trust Allah ﷻ placed in Sulayman (AS). He was given control over winds, over jinn, over extraordinary technology and resources—and then Allah ﷻ said: "Whatever you want to do with all this is entirely up to you. You won't be held accountable for how you use your discretion. I trust you."

فَٱمْنُنْ—"Grant freely." If you believe people will benefit from this technology, give it to them. أَوْ أَمْسِكْ—"Or withhold." If you think releasing certain powers will corrupt people, hold it back. بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ—"Without account." There will be no reckoning for these decisions. The discretion is yours.

This ayah also invites us to reflect on technology itself. What was Sulayman (AS) using the winds for? High-speed travel—what normally took a month could be accomplished in a morning's journey. What was he using jinn for? Undersea construction, extracting treasures from the earth, advanced engineering.

Ask yourself: are we not living in a time where these very technologies are somewhat accessible to us like never before? We have advanced transportation, sophisticated construction, the ability to extract resources from the depths of the earth. But are these technologies in the hands of people with discretion like Sulayman (AS)—people who can benefit humanity or withhold responsibly? Or are we witnessing these technologies in the hands of irresponsible people who are not only plundering and looting the earth, but also causing humanity to suffer more than ever?

It's one thing to extract the earth's treasures to save humanity. It's entirely another to extract those treasures while humanity starves at unprecedented levels. That is unjustifiable.

Another implication: Sulayman (AS) had jinn working as construction laborers alongside human workers. This meant some interaction between humans and jinn was occurring. And where there are corrupt humans in society, they would inevitably start learning things from the jinn. "Hey, you're a jinn? Can you teach me some things? What can you do? Magic?" This mixing led to people learning sorcery and forbidden knowledge. That's why later, as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, the angels Harut and Marut had to come as a test, to distinguish between what was permissible knowledge and what was forbidden magic. These pieces of the larger story are coming together across different surahs.

translation: And indeed, for him is nearness to Us and a good place of return
commentary:
وَإِنَّ لَهُۥ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ

After describing all these worldly blessings and responsibilities, Allah ﷻ concludes: "And indeed, for him is nearness to Us and an excellent place of return."

لَزُلْفَىٰ—closeness, high standing, intimate nearness with Allah ﷻ. Despite all the power, all the technology, all the authority, what ultimately matters is his status with His Master. وَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ—"And an excellent place of return"—a beautiful destination in the afterlife.

This is similar to what was said about Dawud (AS): that he had nearness to Allah ﷻ and an excellent return. True success isn't measured by worldly dominion, but by one's standing with Allah ﷻ.

translation: And remember Our servant Job, when he called to his Lord, "Indeed, Satan has touched me with hardship and torment
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَآ أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ

Now the subject shifts: "And remember Our slave Ayyub (Job), when he called upon his Master: 'Indeed, Shaytan has touched me with exhaustion and torment.'"
