This ayah requires careful understanding. We know that sickness and health both come from Allah ﷻ. Ayyub's (AS) primary test was a severe skin disease—he had rashes, openings in his skin, and according to some narrations, even insects crawling in his wounds. Initially, his wife and family tried to care for him. But as his condition worsened, they eventually couldn't bear it anymore and left him alone.

So why does Ayyub (AS) attribute his suffering to Shaytan? أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ—"Shaytan has touched me with exhaustion and torment."

The answer lies in understanding what happens when a person is severely ill. When someone is sick and their condition keeps deteriorating, they become vulnerable to whispers from Shaytan. "Why is this happening to you? What did you do to deserve this? Try this special dua. Maybe you should use this amulet. I heard if you perform this spiritual exercise or use this potion, it helps."

When a person is sick and desperate, especially when the situation keeps getting worse, they become willing to try anything. This is the نُصْب (exhaustion) and عَذَاب (torment) that Shaytan brings—not the physical illness itself, but the psychological and spiritual assault that accompanies it. The doubts, the desperate thoughts, the temptation to turn to forbidden means for relief.

This is what Ayyub (AS) is describing: the weight of Shaytan's whispers during his trial, making the test even more difficult to bear.

translation: [So he was told], "Strike [the ground] with your foot; this is a [spring for] a cool bath and drink
commentary:
ٱرْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ ۖ هَـٰذَا مُغْتَسَلٌۢ بَارِدٌ وَشَرَابٌ

"Strike with your foot. This is a cool washing place and a drink."

Allah ﷻ commanded Ayyub (AS) to stand and strike the ground with his feet—this to a man who could barely stand. The moment he struck the ground, water gushed forth. This was special water that Allah ﷻ was granting him for healing.

The word مُغْتَسَل  refers to water for bathing, and Allah ﷻ described it as بَارِد —cool—because Ayyub (AS) was burning with fever. But the healing wasn't only external. While the skin disease manifested on the outside, the real infection was within. The virus or bacteria causing his condition was inside his body.

This is why Allah ﷻ told him both to shower with the water and to drink it. The external cleansing would heal what was visible on his skin, and drinking it would cleanse him internally. The cool water would remove the burning torture of the fever. Through this miraculous provision, both dimensions of his suffering were addressed.

translation: And We granted him his family and a like [number] with them as mercy from Us and a reminder for those of understanding
commentary:
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ أَهْلَهُۥ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

"And We granted him his family and the like of them with them as a mercy from Us and a reminder for those of understanding."

After his healing, Allah ﷻ restored Ayyub's family to him. وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ (and the like of them with them) indicates that he was given even more—either more children came along with them, or he married again. All of this was رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا (a mercy from Us).

But this story isn't just about Ayyub (AS). It serves as وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (a powerful reminder for those who truly use their intelligence). For anyone suffering from sickness, this passage deserves deep study and reflection. It shows the difficulty Ayyub (AS) went through, how he kept turning back to Allah ﷻ, how Shaytaan tries to exploit such vulnerable moments, and how even righteous people may say things in their pain that they later regret.

translation: [We said], "And take in your hand a bunch [of grass] and strike with it and do not break your oath." Indeed, We found him patient, an excellent servant. Indeed, he was one repeatedly turning back [to Allah]
commentary:
وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثًا فَٱضْرِب بِّهِۦ وَلَا تَحْنَثْ ۗ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَـٰهُ صَابِرًا ۚ نِّعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ

"And take in your hand a bundle and strike with it and do not break your oath. Indeed, We found him patient. What an excellent slave! Indeed, he was one who repeatedly turned back to Allah."

Remember that in his moment of anger, Ayyub (AS) had sworn to strike his wife a hundred times. Now that she had repented and returned to the truth, Allah ﷻ provided a solution: وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثًا (hold a bundle of straws)—a bundle so large you can't close your fist around it—فَٱضْرِب بِّهِۦ (and strike with it).

Take a hundred straws, tie them together, and strike once with the bundle. This fulfills the oath without causing harm. وَلَا تَحْنَثْ (don't break your oath). The word حِنْث  refers to falling short of fulfilling a pledge or oath you've made.

For ordinary people, when they speak out of emotion, Allah ﷻ doesn't hold them accountable. The Quran already tells us that Allah ﷻ will not hold us to account for just running our mouths in the heat of the moment. But Ayyub (AS) was a prophet, and for the prophets, even a small slip is significant. Therefore, he had to fulfill his oath, and Allah ﷻ revealed to him how to do so in a way that was just.

إِنَّا وَجَدْنَـٰهُ صَابِرًا (We found him patient)—what remarkable patience he demonstrated. نِّعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ (What an excellent slave!)—what an awesome servant of Allah ﷻ he was. إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (He was one who repeatedly turned back to Allah)—the word أَوَّاب emphasizes that he continually, again and again, turned back to his Lord.

translation: And remember Our servants, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob - those of strength and [religious] vision
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ عِبَـٰدَنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَيْدِى وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ

"And remember Our slaves Ibrahim, Ishaq and Yaqub—those who possessed great might and insight."

Allah ﷻ now commands us to remember three mighty prophets together: Ibrahim, Ishaq and Yaqub (AS). They are described as أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَيْدِى وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ (those who possessed great might and insight).

The word أَيْدِي  means hands, but metaphorically refers to strength and power. These were strong messengers. وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ  means insight and foresight—they had deep vision and understanding.

translation: Indeed, We chose them for an exclusive quality: remembrance of the home [of the Hereafter]
commentary:
إِنَّآ أَخْلَصْنَـٰهُم بِخَالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى ٱلدَّارِ

"Indeed, We chose them for an exclusive quality: remembrance of the Home."

أَخْلَصْنَـٰهُم (We purified them, selected them, cleansed them) بِخَالِصَةٍ (with a special cleansing quality)—one particular characteristic that set them apart. What was this quality? ذِكْرَى ٱلدَّارِ (remembrance of the Home)—the constant reminder of the final home, the hereafter.

These prophets would continuously make mention of the last abode. Their lives were oriented toward the eternal home, and they kept reminding others of it as well.

translation: And indeed they are, to Us, among the chosen and outstanding
commentary:
وَإِنَّهُمْ عِندَنَا لَمِنَ ٱلْمُصْطَفَيْنَ ٱلْأَخْيَارِ

"And indeed, they are in Our sight from among the chosen, the best."

عِندَنَا (in Our sight, in Our close company)—they are counted لَمِنَ ٱلْمُصْطَفَيْنَ (among those that were especially chosen). And not just chosen, but ٱلْأَخْيَارِ (the best of them)—the very best.

translation: And remember Ishmael, Elisha and Dhul-Kifl, and all are among the outstanding
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَٱلْيَسَعَ وَذَا ٱلْكِفْلِ ۖ وَكُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلْأَخْيَارِ

"And remember Ismail, Al-Yasa and Dhal-Kifl—and all are from the best."

Three more prophets are mentioned: Ismail (AS), Al-Yasa (AS)—who is considered to have come later, a contemporary relief after another prophet—and Dhal-Kifl (AS). We don't know much detail about Al-Yasa or Dhal-Kifl from the Quran itself.

Perhaps Allah ﷻ mentions these names without elaborating on their stories because it creates longing. When you meet them in Jannah, you'll want to ask: "What was your story? Tell me all about it." You'll want to get to know them more and more. Each one has a different account, a different journey.

وَكُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلْأَخْيَارِ (and all of them are from the very best).

translation: This is a reminder. And indeed, for the righteous is a good place of return
commentary:
هَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ

"This is a reminder. And indeed, for the righteous is the best place of return."

هَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ (This is all a remembrance and a reminder)—what has just been mentioned about these prophets serves as a powerful reminder for us.

وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (and for those who really protected themselves, who were conscious of Allah ﷻ) لَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ (the best possible return, the best place to go back to). The best possible place to return was mentioned for the prophets one after the other. Now you're being told: you get to go hang out with the prophets. You get the best return also.

translation: Gardens of perpetual residence, whose doors will be opened to them
commentary:
جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ مُّفَتَّحَةً لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبُ

"Gardens of Eden with the gates widely opened for them."

جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ (Gardens of Eden)—how incredible is that? مُّفَتَّحَةً لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبُ (the doors are already widely opened for them).

When you have a guest coming and you know they're arriving, you leave the door open. Allah ﷻ says the gates of Jannah are already open for the believers. The word مُّفَتَّحَةً doesn't mean they're just open—it means they're wide open. It's not like the owner accidentally pushed the door and it didn't close all the way. Both panels of the door are flung wide open, as if calling out: "Come on in! This one has your name on it."

مُّفَتَّحَةً لَّهُمُ (especially for them, the doors are being held open). The gates are being held open by the angels. By the way, the exact opposite is true of Jahannam—the gates of Hell are always closed. That's part of Allah's mercy. The gates of Jahannam remain closed, and when people arrive there, then they're opened.

translation: Reclining within them, they will call therein for abundant fruit and drink
commentary:
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ وَشَرَابٍ

"Reclining therein, they will call for abundant fruit and drink."

مُتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا (reclining in it, relaxing in it). You can just imagine them already. Since the doors are open, the mind wanders: what's going to happen inside those doors?

When you go to somebody's house, what's the first thing you do? You sit down. The host says: "Relax, make yourself comfortable." 

But this isn't someone else's house—this is your own house. When you go to someone else's home as a guest, you don't immediately start asking for things. You don't say, "Do you have any ketchup?" You wouldn't normally do that at somebody else's house. But in your own home, what do you do? You call out for what you want. You ask your kids, "Is there any chocolate in the fridge?"

That's the comfort level being described here. يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا (they'll start asking for stuff). They'll sit there and say, "Let's have some food." بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ (bring lots of fruit—abundant amounts of it). It starts coming. وَشَرَابٍ (and the drinks)—the drinks start flowing.

translation: And with them will be women limiting [their] glances and of equal age
commentary:
وَعِندَهُمْ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ أَتْرَابٌ

"And with them will be those who restrain their glances, equal in age."

وَعِندَهُمْ (and in their company) قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ (those shy spouses who keep lowering their eyes). They sit next to them, أَتْرَابٌ (and they are compatible, similar in age and temperament).

Just because someone is beautiful doesn't mean they have a good personality. Physical beauty doesn't guarantee compatibility. But in Jannah, these companions are أَتْرَاب—perfectly compatible. They're the same age as you. They like what you like. They don't like what you don't like. They're a perfect fit.

Sometimes such compatibility isn't possible in this world. Even among the Sahaba (RA), there were cases of incompatibility. We read about the lack of compatibility between Zaynab bint Jahsh (RA) and Zayd ibn Harithah (RA). That's okay—it happens in this world.

But in Jannah, there is total and absolute compatibility, and it's not artificial. We see movies and soap operas about relationships where everything is perfect when people first meet, but things start going sour after a while. In Jannah, you're with your spouse forever—and that compatibility never fades.

When you spend a lifetime together in this world, people can get on each other's nerves. Parents and grandparents who've been together for decades develop ways of speaking to each other. But the أَتْرَاب in Jannah represent the opposite—everything remains in perfect compatibility. Everything is smooth.

translation: This is what you, [the righteous], are promised for the Day of Account
commentary:
هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ

"This is what you are promised for the Day of Account."

هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ (This is what you were promised) لِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (for the Day of Resurrection, the Day of Accounting).

translation: Indeed, this is Our provision; for it there is no depletion
commentary:
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَرِزْقُنَا مَا لَهُۥ مِن نَّفَادٍ

"Indeed, this is Our provision—it will never have an end."

إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَرِزْقُنَا (This is Our provision). مَا لَهُۥ مِن نَّفَادٍ (It does not have any end to it whatsoever, any expiration to it).

Remember earlier in the Quran, Allah ﷻ told us: "If the oceans were turned into ink to write the words of my Master, the oceans would run dry—they would run out, they would expire—but the words of Allah ﷻ would not."

There's no expiration on this provision. The enjoyment they experience never runs out. In this world, sometimes you have something really good and you think, "That hit the spot"—but it's finished. Not in Jannah. There's no نَفَاد —no running out.

translation: This [is so]. But indeed, for the transgressors is an evil place of return
commentary:
هَـٰذَا ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلطَّـٰغِينَ لَشَرَّ مَـَٔابٍ

"This is so. But indeed, for the transgressors is the worst place of return."

Now the tone shifts dramatically. هَـٰذَا (that's the case for the believers). وَإِنَّ لِلطَّـٰغِينَ (but for the rebellious, the transgressors) لَشَرَّ مَـَٔابٍ (the worst possible place to return to).

translation: Hell, which they will [enter to] burn, and wretched is the resting place
commentary:
جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ

"Jahannam—they will burn therein. And wretched is that resting place!"

جَهَنَّمَ —and they'll be يَصْلَوْنَهَا (thrown into it, burned in it). فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (What a horrible cradle, what a horrible resting place it is). 

The word مِهَاد refers to something that cradles or hugs you, like a bed. What a horrible bed that envelops them.

translation: This - so let them taste it - is scalding water and [foul] purulence
commentary:
هَـٰذَا فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ

"This! So let them taste it: scalding water and purulence."

هَـٰذَا (this is it). فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ (Then they should taste it).

The sentence structure is striking. "Let them taste" is stated, but then the sentence cuts and starts over: حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ (scalding water and purulence). Just as the taste doesn't go smoothly down the throat, the sentence itself cuts and restarts.

حَمِيم means boiling hot water—so hot that large bubbles form on its surface. وَغَسَّاق refers to bodily fluids that have left the body: pus, blood, tears, sweat. Some scholars say it especially refers to pus. This is what they're made to drink.

translation: And other [punishments] of its type [in various] kinds
commentary:
وَءَاخَرُ مِن شَكْلِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجٌ

"And other punishments of its type, paired together."

وَءَاخَرُ (and another drink) مِن شَكْلِهِۦٓ (that can't even be described, but something like it) أَزْوَٰجٌ (categories of them, whole arrays).

A whole array of horrible drinks—cocktails of horrible drinks—laid out for them.

translation: [Its inhabitants will say], "This is a company bursting in with you. No welcome for them. Indeed, they will burn in the Fire
commentary:
هَـٰذَا فَوْجٌ مُّقْتَحِمٌ مَّعَكُمْ ۖ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ صَالُوا۟ ٱلنَّارِ

"This is a company bursting in with you! No welcome for them! Indeed, they will burn in the Fire."

Then an announcement is made to those already in Hell: هَـٰذَا فَوْجٌ مُّقْتَحِمٌ مَّعَكُمْ (Here's a whole multitude of people coming to join you).

In this world, we talk about overcrowded prison systems. Imagine the prison is already overcrowded, everybody's stuffed together, and the announcement is made: "Here's another huge bundle of people being squished in with you."

The word مُّقْتَحِمٌ comes from اقْتَحَمَ , meaning to jump quickly into something. But how can somebody leap into fire? They should be dragged into it. Yet Allah ﷻ tells us elsewhere in the Quran that they're pushed so hard that it looks like they're leaping in.

The guardian of Hell opens the gate and makes the announcement: more coming through. They're shoved in forcefully.

Those already at the bottom cry out: لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِهِمْ (They're not welcome here!). In Arabic, when someone comes to your house, you say مَرْحَبًا —welcome. But here they say لَا مَرْحَبًۢا (no welcome). There's no room for them here. Why are they coming? Why do we need more?

إِنَّهُمْ صَالُوا۟ ٱلنَّارِ (They're going to be thrown into the fire)—whether you welcome them or not.

translation: They will say, "Nor you! No welcome for you. You, [our leaders], brought this upon us, and wretched is the settlement
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ بَلْ أَنتُمْ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِكُمْ ۖ أَنتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ

"They said: 'Rather, no welcome for you! You brought this upon us, so wretched is the settlement!'"

The people being pushed in from above heard those at the bottom say they're not welcome. Now they respond: قَالُوا۟ بَلْ أَنتُمْ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِكُمْ (They said: no, you're not welcome either!).

أَنتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنَا (You're the reason we're here! You're the one who set this whole place up for us). You're the ancestors whose traditions we followed. You're the elders we wanted to hold onto so strongly. You're those elders who, at the beginning of this surah, told the Muslims and anyone who would listen: "Don't listen to him! Get out of here! Why are you listening to him?"

You set the stage for us. فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ (What a horrible place to remain this is!).

translation: They will say, "Our Lord, whoever brought this upon us - increase for him double punishment in the Fire
commentary:
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا مَن قَدَّمَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا فَزِدْهُ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا فِى ٱلنَّارِ

"They said: 'Our Lord, whoever brought this upon us, increase for him double punishment in the Fire!'"

Then those who followed cry out: قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا مَن قَدَّمَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا (Our Master, whoever got ahead of us in this, whoever took the lead, whoever is in the first ranks of animosity against Islam)—those people, فَزِدْهُ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا فِى ٱلنَّارِ (give them double the punishment in the Fire!).

translation: And they will say, "Why do we not see men whom we used to count among the worst
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا لَنَا لَا نَرَىٰ رِجَالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَشْرَارِ

"And they will say: 'What is wrong with us that we do not see men whom we used to count among the worst?'"

The sequencing of the Quran here is incredible. In the previous surah, a conversation happened in Jannah. Friends were hanging out together and one asked, "Where did so-and-so go?" He found out his old friend ended up in Hellfire.

Now there's an equivalent conversation happening in Jahannam—the same type of exchange, but flipped. It's happening among people burning together.

Remember how in Jannah they greeted one another, asking each other questions, recognizing ancestors and relatives? "You're my grandfather who became Muslim! You're the one who migrated! You lived during the time of the colonizers—how was it back then?"

It was a beautiful meet-and-greet. But here, another group is coming, and instead of being greeted warmly, they're told: "It's your fault."

Now these leaders—the same ones who were so dismissive at the beginning of this surah—say: مَا لَنَا لَا نَرَىٰ رِجَالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَشْرَارِ (What's wrong with us? We can't see certain men whom we used to count among the worst people).

translation: Is it [because] we took them in ridicule, or has [our] vision turned away from them
commentary:
أَتَّخَذْنَـٰهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا أَمْ زَاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ

"Did we take them in ridicule, or have our eyes missed them?"

أَتَّخَذْنَـٰهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا (Did we used to make fun of them?) أَمْ زَاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (Or have our eyes just missed them? Where did they go? Can't find them here).

They're just left with the question unanswered. They're abandoned with their confusion.

translation: Indeed, that is truth - the quarreling of the people of the Fire
commentary:
إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخَاصُمُ أَهْلِ ٱلنَّارِ

"Indeed, that is truth—the quarreling of the people of the Fire."

Allah ﷻ declares: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَحَقٌّ (This is for real). You think this is just a story to scare you? This is actually happening. This debate is going to take place.

تَخَاصُمُ أَهْلِ ٱلنَّارِ (the vicious arguing against each other of the people of Fire). By the way, when the people of Jannah wondered about their missing companion, their request was granted and they saw what happened to him. But here in Hell, there's no mention of anything being granted. They just can't see certain people. Where did they go? The question hangs in the air.

Between the lines of these two conversations—one in Jannah and one in Jahannam, described in these two surahs—we're being told: you will be in one of these two conversations. You pick. That's the choice being placed before us.

translation: Say, [O Muhammad], "I am only a warner, and there is not any deity except Allah, the One, the Prevailing
commentary:
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ

"Say: 'I am only a warner, and there is no deity except Allah, the One, the Prevailing.'"

قُلْ (Tell them) إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مُنذِرٌ (I'm just here to warn you. I'm just a warner).

وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ (No one is to be worshiped and obeyed except Allah ﷻ) ٱلْوَٰحِدُ (the One) ٱلْقَهَّارُ (the Dominant, the one who cannot be avoided, the Undeniable, the one who has ultimate authority).

translation: Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the Exalted in Might, the Perpetual Forgiver
commentary:
رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ

"Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the Exalted in Might, the Perpetual Forgiver."

رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا (The Master of the skies and the earth and whatever lies between them).

ٱلْعَزِيزُ (The ultimate authority)—He has the authority because He has the power to punish. But He is also ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ (the one who continually and repeatedly keeps on forgiving). These two divine names together capture the balance: absolute power paired with endless mercy.

translation: Say, "It is great news
commentary:
قُلْ هُوَ نَبَؤٌا۟ عَظِيمٌ

"Say: 'It is great news.'"

قُلْ هُوَ نَبَؤٌا۟ عَظِيمٌ (Tell them: this is huge news). What you've just been told—the scenes you've been shown—are no small thing. This is tremendous news.

translation: From which you turn away
commentary:
أَنتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ

"From which you turn away."

أَنتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ (Yet you're still ignoring it. You're not taking it seriously. You're turning away from it).

translation: I had no knowledge of the exalted assembly [of angels] when they were disputing [the creation of Adam]
commentary:
مَا كَانَ لِىَ مِنْ عِلْمٍۭ بِٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰٓ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ

"I had no knowledge of the exalted assembly when they disputed."

مَا كَانَ لِىَ مِنْ عِلْمٍۭ بِٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰٓ (I don't have any knowledge of the highest assembly, the highest angels) إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (when they have debates among themselves).

This teaches us something important about angels. The popular understanding is that ٱلْمَلَأُ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ refers to the highest angels. And even among the highest angels, there's debate happening.

We learn that angels don't just robotically obey Allah ﷻ. Yes, whatever Allah ﷻ commands them, they do—but there's more to them. They have personalities. They can think. They have intelligence. They have opinions. They never disobey Allah ﷻ—that's certain—but they do have opinions of their own, and they can process things.

There are differences of opinion among angels. One might think: "Judgment should just come right now. These people should just be destroyed. Look at what they did to Muhammad ﷺ today. They should just be killed." Another might respond: "No, no, there's more time. There's more time. Allah's plan hasn't fully unfolded yet."

They're like soldiers having a conversation about when the general is going to send them into action. The Prophet ﷺ says: I have no access to that conversation. I don't know about the debates happening among the angels.

translation: It has not been revealed to me except that I am a clear warner
commentary:
إِن يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ إِلَّآ أَنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

"It is only revealed to me that I am a clear warner."

إِن يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ إِلَّآ (The only thing sent to me, the only thing revealed to me is nothing but this:) أَنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (that I am exclusively a clear, obvious warner).

translation: [So mention] when your Lord said to the angels, "Indeed, I am going to create a human being from clay
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن طِينٍ

"When your Lord said to the angels: 'Indeed, I am going to create a human being from clay.'"

Now we come to the final conversation of the surah, and this is the last time in the Quran that the story of Adam (AS) will be repeated in detail.

The wisdom of placing this story here, at this point in the sequence, is profound. We just witnessed the end of humanity—we saw where humanity ends up if they follow in the footsteps of Shaytaan. Now Allah ﷻ takes us all the way back to the beginning of humanity, to show us where this entire journey began and how people ended up following the very thing their first father was warned against.

Remember the conversation in Hell: "You were ahead of us. You are our fathers. We ended up following you." In this world, people argue: "We're only doing what our tradition says. We're following our ancestors."

Allah ﷻ responds: if you really want to follow tradition, just follow Adam (AS). If you want to follow a father, then follow your real father, Adam (AS). There's a legacy you can actually hold onto. That's the message being conveyed as the surah prepares to conclude.

translation: So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My [created] soul, then fall down to him in prostration
commentary:
فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ

We've reached the end of Surah Sad, where Allah ﷻ continues the narrative He began earlier about the creation of Adam (AS). Allah ﷻ recalls what He said to the angels: "I am about to create a mortal being from clay, from wet soil."

The word سَوَّيْتُهُۥ  - "when I finally mold him" - carries the meaning of balancing something or putting finishing touches on it. You first create something, then you refine it. Taswiyah is the act of refining, balancing, making sure every part of it is even and complete. The Quran describes this pattern elsewhere when Allah ﷻ says He creates, then puts finishing touches on His creation.

After these finishing touches are complete, Allah ﷻ says He will blow into this creation من رُّوحِى - "of My ruh." The term ruh is often translated as "soul" or "spirit," but it represents something far more significant. It's one of Allah's special creations, a divine element that He chose to place within humanity.

Then comes the command: "Fall before him in prostration." This establishes the unique honor of the human being from the very moment of creation.

translation: So the angels prostrated - all of them entirely
commentary:
فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ

The angels responded with complete obedience - all of them, without exception, fell into prostration. The combination of كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ - "all of them without exception" - is an unusual pairing in Arabic. This linguistic emphasis tells us there were absolutely no exceptions among the angels who performed this prostration.

This detail is crucial for understanding what follows. Iblis could not possibly have been a fallen angel according to the Quran. He must have been from the jinn, as explicitly mentioned in Surah Al-Kahf: كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ  - "he was from among the jinn."

translation: Except Iblees; he was arrogant and became among the disbelievers
commentary:
إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ ٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

The word إِلَّآ  - "except" - needs careful understanding here. It doesn't simply mean "except Iblis." The phrase إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ  tells us "not so Iblis" - he didn't do so.

Then we find the verb ٱسْتَكْبَرَ  - "he showed arrogance." This isn't just ordinary pride. The form istakbara means he tried to show his own greatness, he attempted to be arrogant, he demonstrated superiority. This was an active choice, a deliberate display.

The ayah concludes: وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ  - "and he had been from those who disbelieved." The verb كَانَ  indicates a past, continuous state. He had already been from the disbelievers. This wasn't a momentary lapse but revealed something that was already present within him.

translation: [Allah] said, "O Iblees, what prevented you from prostrating to that which I created with My hands? Were you arrogant [then], or were you [already] among the haughty
commentary:
قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَن تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَىَّ ۖ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْعَالِينَ

Allah ﷻ asks Iblis directly: "What prevented you from prostrating to the one I created بِيَدَىَّ  - with both My hands?"

This phrase "both My hands" has sparked theological debates throughout Islamic history. Rather than engaging in speculative discussions about the nature of Allah's attributes, we should focus on what we can understand from the text. The Quran mentions "hands" in different contexts - sometimes plural as in "We created the sky with Our hands," sometimes singular as in "the hand of Allah is above their hands," and here specifically dual, "both My hands."

One meaningful reflection on this phrase relates to the two dimensions of Adam's creation. Allah ﷻ owns all of creation - both the world of khalq (physical creation) and the world of amr (divine command). The only creature made from both these dimensions is humanity. Both hands could refer to this dual nature: Allah ﷻ created the human being from the dimension of khalq (the physical clay) and the dimension of amr (the divine ruh that was blown into him).

Then Allah ﷻ poses two questions: أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ  - "Have you shown arrogance?" Or أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْعَالِينَ - "are you truly from those who are elevated, those who deserve that kind of high status?"

This is powerful questioning. Are you just displaying arrogance, or are you actually convinced you belong among the elevated ones? Do you think you have real superiority, or is this just a show?

translation: He said, "I am better than him. You created me from fire and created him from clay
commentary:
قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ

Look at how defiantly Iblis speaks directly to Allahﷻ : أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ  - "I am better than him."

His reasoning? خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ - "You created me from fire, and You created him from clay."

Allah ﷻ already mentioned He created humans from clay. But then He placed His ruh in them. The clay itself wasn't enough - if it were, humans would be no different from other creatures also made from earthly elements. What elevates humanity is the ruh that was blown into Adam (AS), declaring the supremacy of the human being.

Iblis doesn't acknowledge the existence of the ruh. Perhaps he doesn't know about it, or perhaps he doesn't care to recognize it. All he sees is طِين  - clay. He doesn't see what's so great about the human being because in his view, humans are just made of dirt.

This should sound familiar. In our modern age, when we don't acknowledge the existence of the ruh anymore, what do we see? We see ourselves as just another animal. We don't recognize what's special about the human being. Some even compare human behavior to animals to justify certain actions, saying "it's found in nature" among certain species. This is the ultimate denial of the soul - modeling ourselves after animals, forgetting that the human being is honored.

The Quran states: وَلَقَدْ كَرَّمْنَا بَنِىٓ ءَادَمَ  - "We have certainly honored the children of Adam." This honor doesn't come from being at the center of the universe or from our physical composition. It comes from the ruh that Allah ﷻ placed within us. That's what we must honor in ourselves and in each other.

Shaytan's entire mission is to make humanity forget this honor. He wants us to forget the ruh, to think we're just clay, just physical beings. He calls us only to what our clay existence wants - food, shelter, physical gratification, all the temptations this world offers. He wants us to reduce ourselves, to humiliate ourselves, and not acknowledge our souls.

translation: [Allah] said, "Then get out of Paradise, for indeed, you are expelled
commentary:
قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ responds with immediate expulsion: فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا  - "Get out of here."

The word رَجِيمٌ carries two meanings. It means "cursed," but rajm also means to pelt with stones. So رَجِيمٌ is the one who is cursed and the one who gets pelted. This dual meaning captures both the spiritual state of being removed from Allah's mercy and the punishment that awaits.

translation: And indeed, upon you is My curse until the Day of Recompense
commentary:
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ

The curse isn't temporary - وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِىٓ  - "upon you exclusively is My curse." The word عَلَيْكَ emphasizes "upon you" specifically, personally.

This curse extends إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ  - "until the Day of Judgment." Every moment until that final day, the curse remains.

translation: He said, "My Lord, then reprieve me until the Day they are resurrected
commentary:
قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ

Iblis responds with a request: رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ  - "My Master, then give me time, let me stay around, wait for me."

The request is specific: إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ  - "until the day they are raised," meaning the Day of Resurrection when all humanity will be brought back to life.

## Ayah 80-81

قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ
إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ

Allah ﷻ grants this request: فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ  - "Then you are from those who are given time."

But notice the specific wording: إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ - "until the day of the known time." Allah ﷻ didn't say "until the day they are raised" as Iblis requested. He said "until the known time" - a time known to Allah ﷻ alone.

translation: [Allah] said, "So indeed, you are of those reprieved
Until the Day of the time well-known
[Iblees] said, "By your might, I will surely mislead them all
commentary:
قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

Now Iblis makes his vow: فَبِعِزَّتِكَ  - "I swear by Your might, by Your authority." This is remarkable - he's swearing by Allah's own authority, the same authority that just granted him time to exist. He acknowledges Allah's power even as he plots against humanity.

His declaration: لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ  - "I will absolutely lead them astray, all of them, everyone together."

The word أُغْوِيَنَّ  means to mislead, to take someone off the path, to dilute their understanding. He's declaring total war on humanity.

translation: Except, among them, Your chosen servants
commentary:
إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ

But then comes the exception: إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ  - "except Your slaves among them who have been granted sincerity."

This connects beautifully to an earlier passage in this very Surah. When describing those who will be protected, Allah ﷻ said: إِلَّا عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ  - "except the sincere slaves of Allah."

Notice the word ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ  - those who have been granted sincerity, those who have been made sincere. Iblis himself admits this. He's saying: "The slaves to whom You intervene and give sincerity - I won't be able to do anything about them." He acknowledges that sincere devotion to Allah ﷻ is beyond his reach.

## Ayah 84-85

قَالَ فَٱلْحَقُّ وَٱلْحَقَّ أَقُولُ
لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

Allah ﷻ responds: فَٱلْحَقُّ وَٱلْحَقَّ أَقُولُ - "The truth, and the truth is what I say."

His declaration is absolute: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ  - "I will absolutely fill Hell." With whom? مِنكَ  - "with you" - Iblis himself first.

Then وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ  - "and whoever among them follows you, all together."

We've already seen the scene of Hell earlier in this Surah. Now we're taken back to where this saga began, completing the narrative circle. This is how the story progresses - showing us both the origin of the conflict and its ultimate conclusion.

translation: [Allah] said, "The truth [is My oath], and the truth I say
[That] I will surely fill Hell with you and those of them that follow you all together
Say, [O Muhammad], "I do not ask you for the Qur'an any payment, and I am not of the pretentious
commentary:
قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ

After this intense narrative about Iblis and his schemes, Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to make a declaration: قُلْ  - "Say."

مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ  - "I'm not asking you for any compensation." The message of Islam, the warning about Shaytan, the reminder of truth - all of this comes with no price tag.

وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ  - "And I am not from those who pretend, who manufacture things."

The word تَكَلُّف  and its related form مُتَكَلِّف  need proper understanding. The root concept involves trying to show something above one's natural ability, attempting to do something beyond what you can genuinely handle.

When someone takes on a burden they cannot truly bear, that's takalluf. For example, Allah ﷻ told all the messengers: لَا تَتَكَلَّفُوا . This doesn't mean "don't work hard" - it means don't artificially take on a load you know you can't handle. If a guest wants to come late at night and you're not ready, don't say "Yes, of course, come over" when you can't genuinely host them, because once they stay until 3 AM, you'll end up angry at them. That's not a good deed. You didn't do anyone a favor by saying yes and then being resentful.

In the context of revelation, the disbelievers accused the Prophet ﷺ of being a mutakallif - someone pretending to be a messenger, working really hard to make up this Quran, forcing himself to produce something beyond his natural capability. They knew he wasn't a poet. Suddenly producing this eloquent Quran seemed like takalluf to them.

Allah ﷻ instructs him to declare: "I'm not pretending. I'm not manufacturing this. This isn't me trying really hard to be something I'm not. This revelation comes from Allah ﷻ, not from my artificial efforts."

translation: It is but a reminder to the worlds
commentary:
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

What is this Quran? إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ  - "This is nothing but a reminder."

For whom? لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ  - "for all nations, for all worlds, for all of humanity."

Not a message for one tribe, one era, one people. A universal reminder for everyone, everywhere, across all time.

translation: And you will surely know [the truth of] its information after a time
commentary:
وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُۥ بَعْدَ حِينٍۭ

The Surah concludes with a powerful promise: وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ  - "And you will absolutely come to know."

Know what? نَبَأَهُۥ  - "its news, its reality, its truth."

When? بَعْدَ حِينٍۭ  - "after a while, after some time."

Earlier in this very Surah, Allah ﷻ said: هَـٰذَا نَبَأٌ عَظِيمٌ - "This is tremendous news." What is it? The reality of the Hereafter, the truth of resurrection, the scenes of Paradise and Hell that this Surah has described.

You will get to see it with your own eyes. You will experience its reality. After just a little while, you will know. The questioning in the grave, the Day of Judgment, the fulfillment of every promise - it's all coming. Just wait a little while, and you will see.

This is how Surah Sad concludes - taking us from the story of creation, through the rebellion of Iblis, to the ultimate reality that awaits us all. The reminder is universal, the message is clear, and the time to see its truth is closer than we think.
